doc.title: Lump-sum issuance — allocate the basket
doc.event: Ida Corp. sells 1,000 shares of $10-par common and 500 shares of $10-par preferred for a single lump-sum payment of $30,000. Prepare the issuance entry.

variants.label: Both FVs known: common $24,000, preferred $8,000 — split by ratio

entries.caption: Proportional method: allocate $30,000 by relative fair values (24k : 8k)

lines.label: the lump sum
lines.trap: The lump sum rarely equals the sum of the fair values — that's WHY allocation exists.

lines.label: 1,000 × $10 par
lines.why: Common's allocated share = 30,000 × 24/32 = 22,500. Of that, par capital is 1,000 × $10.
lines.trap: Allocate FIRST, split par second — reversing the order scrambles both classes.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.why: Common's excess over its par capital.
lines.trap: Each class keeps its own PIC account — never pool the excess.

lines.label: 500 × $10 par
lines.why: Preferred's allocated share = 30,000 × 8/32 = 7,500; par piece is 5,000.
lines.trap: Preferred follows the same par mechanics as common — different class, identical machinery.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Preferred
lines.trap: Check: 10,000 + 12,500 + 5,000 + 2,500 = 30,000. Allocations must consume the lump sum exactly.

computationPaths.narration: Relative-FV split: total FV = 24,000 + 8,000 = 32,000. Common takes 24/32 of the $30,000 (22,500); preferred takes 8/32 (7,500). Then each class runs normal par mechanics on its slice.

variants.label: Only common's FV known ($24,000) — preferred gets the leftover

entries.caption: Incremental method: known FV first, remainder to the unknown class

lines.label: the lump sum
lines.trap: Method choice is forced by the facts — you don't get to pick incremental when both FVs are known.

lines.label: par piece of the known 24,000
lines.why: Common (the known one) takes its full fair value: 24,000. Par piece: 10,000.
lines.trap: The KNOWN security anchors at its FV; only the unknown one flexes.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.why: Common's excess on its full FV.
lines.trap: Note this differs from the proportional answer (12,500) — same deal, different information, different numbers. Exams exploit exactly that.

lines.label: par piece of the residual
lines.why: Preferred gets what's left: 30,000 − 24,000 = 6,000. Par piece: 5,000.
lines.trap: The residual can be unflattering (here just 1,000 above par) — that's the price of not having a market quote.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Preferred
lines.why: The last dollar of the lump sum, accounted for.
lines.trap: Same check-figure discipline: the five lines must reconstruct 30,000 exactly.

computationPaths.narration: Known-first: common anchors at 24,000; preferred is the plug (30,000 − 24,000 = 6,000). Each slice then splits par/excess normally.

memorize.body: Both FVs known → proportional: each class gets lump × (its FV ÷ total FV). One FV known → incremental: known class at its FV, other class gets the residual.
Always reconcile: the credits across both classes must sum exactly to the lump sum. It's a free arithmetic check the exam gives you.

questions.prompt: A $30,000 lump sum buys common (FV $24,000) and preferred (FV $8,000). Under the proportional method, common's allocation is…

distractors.feedback: 24,000 is common's standalone FAIR VALUE — the ratio ingredient, not the allocation. The basket sold for less than the FVs sum to, so common gets 30,000 × 24/32 = 22,500. (Anchoring at full FV is the INCREMENTAL method, used only when the other FV is unknown.)

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Preferred

build.scaffold: Allocate before you journalize: how many fair values do you know? Two → ratio split. One → known at FV, other gets the leftover. THEN run par mechanics inside each slice.
